data.title: Hang Seng Index Futures Extend Bearish Momentum, RHB
data.description:
Hang Seng Index Futures (HSIF) extended their decline for a third consecutive session as selling pressure persisted, prompting RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) to maintain its bearish trading stance on the contract.

In a trading note, RHB Research Institute said the Hang Seng Index Futures (HSIF) fell 523 points to close at 25,132 points during the day session. The contract opened at 25,642 points and slipped to an intraday low of 24,887 points before settling lower.

However, the evening session saw some recovery, with the futures adding 402 points to last trade at 25,534 points.

Despite the rebound, the research house said the overall price action remains negative, although strong psychological support is expected around the 25,000-point level. The index may move sideways along this immediate support in the near term.

RHB Research noted that a bullish reversal candlestick could signal a potential shift in momentum if it forms near the support level. Should the support break, the correction could deepen towards 24,500 points.

“Maintain short position,” the research house said, adding that traders should retain the short position initiated at the close of Feb 26 at 26,367 points.

To manage risk, the stop-loss level has been set at 28,600 points.

Looking ahead, the first support level is pegged at 24,500 points, followed by 23,500 points, while immediate resistance is seen at 26,600 points and 27,500 points.

data.title: COMEX Gold Eyes Upside As Bullish Momentum Strengthens
data.description:
COMEX gold rose US$11 on Wednesday, closing at US$5,134.70 as bullish momentum builds, according to RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research).

The precious metal opened at US$5,110.00, touched a session low of US$5,092.80, and reached a high of US$5,218.30 before settling above its 20-day simple moving average, signalling strength among buyers.

RHB Research noted that the gold market is positioned to resume its upward movement towards the US$5,500 level. In the event of renewed selling pressure, the commodity could retest the US$5,000 support, with US$4,800 marked as the stop-loss threshold for long positions initiated on February 11 at US$5,098.50.

Immediate resistance is anticipated at US$5,500, followed by US$6,000, while support levels are cited at US$5,000 and US$4,800.

The research house maintained a positive trading bias, advising investors to hold existing long positions while monitoring potential market fluctuations.

data.title: Stock Picks: VSTECS And Powerwell
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd has identified VSTECS Bhd and Powerwell Holdings Bhd as potential breakout candidates, supported by strengthening technical indicators and improving trading momentum.

For VSTECS, the research house said the stock is setting up for a potential bullish breakout as it attempts to move above its immediate resistance level. The counter’s 21-day simple moving average (SMA) continues to slope upwards, indicating that the short-term trend remains positive.

Rising trading volume alongside the share price also suggests growing market participation. A close above the RM5 resistance may attract fresh bullish momentum, potentially pushing the stock towards RM5.25, with the next resistance seen at RM5.50. However, a drop below the RM4.75 support level could weaken the bullish outlook and trigger a deeper pullback.

Meanwhile, Powerwell is also positioning for a breakout as the stock tests its immediate resistance with a bullish candlestick formation.

RHB Research noted that positive momentum is supported by rising trading volume, signalling strengthening buying interest. A breakout above the RM0.65 level may pave the way for the stock to retest RM0.70, followed by RM0.75.

On the downside, the research house cautioned that a fall below the RM0.60 support could dampen market sentiment and open the door for a correction.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Slides At Midday As Wall Street Weakness Weighs
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia ended the morning session lower on March 4, tracking overnight losses on Wall Street amid mounting concerns over global oil and gas supply disruptions and inflation risks linked to the ongoing Middle East crisis.

At 12:30pm, the benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) fell 9.56 points, or 0.55%, to 1,702.39 from its close of 1,711.95 on March 3.

Broader indices were also in the red, with the FBM 70 dropped 0.85% to 17,210.78, the FBM EMAS slid 0.67% to 12,468.91, the FBM SHA eased 0.4% to 12,123.20, while the FBM 100 declined 0.63% to 1,005.39.

Market breadth was firmly negative too, with decliners outpacing gainers 795 to 233. A total of 439 counters were unchanged, 1,155 untraded and 10 suspended.

Among the most actively traded stocks were energy-linked counters, with Bumi Armada Bhd leading volumes at 67.9 million shares traded, slipping one sen to 37 sen. Velesto Energy Bhd shed half a sen to 32.5 sen, while Zetrix AI Bhd fell two sen to 77 sen.

Hengyuan Refining Company Bhd stood out, rising 17 sen to RM1.52 on heavy volume of 32.8 million shares, making it one of the session’s top gainers.

On the blue-chip front, Nestle (M) Bhd gained 70 sen to RM108.20, while PPB Group Bhd advanced 36 sen to RM10.96. United Plantations Bhd and Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd also edged higher by 20 sen and 18 sen, respectively, to RM29.80 and RM30.60.

Leading losers included BLD Plantation Bhd, down 68 sen to RM14.62, Heineken Malaysia Bhd, which fell 66 sen to RM23.04, and Hong Leong Bank Bhd, which slipped 48 sen to RM22.92. Carlsberg Brewery Malaysia Bhd and Vitrox Corporation Bhd were also among notable decliners as both counters dropped 34 sen and 27 sen, respectively.

With external uncertainties clouding sentiment, investors remained cautious, trimming exposure amid concerns that elevated energy prices could rekindle global inflationary pressures.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Rebounds At Midday As Buying Returns
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia rebounded at midday on March 5, with the benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI rising 11.21 points as buying interest returned across key heavyweights and broader market indices.

At 12:30pm, the FBM KLCI stood at 1,709.43, up 0.66% from the morning session. Other indices also traded higher, with the FBM EMAS climbing 65.56 points to 12,500.03 and the FBM ACE edging up 4.71 points to 4,510.53.

The broader market was positive, with 523 gainers outpacing 431 losers, while 476 counters were unchanged. Market activity remained active with turnover reaching 1.45 billion shares valued at RM1.33 billion.

On the gainers’ list, Nestlé (M) Bhd advanced RM1 to RM108, while Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd added 60 sen to RM31.10 and Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd climbed 27 sen to RM7.67.

On the downside, Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd fell 38 sen to RM20.88, while Fraser & Neave Holdings Bhd slipped 28 sen to RM33.62 and IOI Properties Group Bhd eased 11 sen to RM3.29.

Overall sentiment improved through the morning session as investors rotated into selected heavyweights and active counters, lifting the broader market into positive territory by midday.

data.title: UTDPLT Leads Gains As HEIM Tumbles
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia saw UTDPLT emerge as the top gainer, climbing 60 sen to RM30.20 on active trading of 266,300 shares.

PPB followed, rising 30 sen to RM10.90 with 1,060,100 shares changing hands, while HENGYUAN gained 19 sen to RM1.54 on heavy volume of 59,359,400 shares. MPI and SUNWAY also recorded modest increases of 8 sen each to RM30.50 and RM5.63 respectively.

On the downside, HEIM led losses, slipping 90 sen to RM22.80 with 332,900 shares traded. Nestle fell 50 sen to RM107.00 on 66,500 shares, while ALLIANZ dropped 38 sen to RM21.36.

HLBANK and CARLSBG also retreated, down 34 sen and 30 sen to RM23.06 and RM17.06 respectively, with volume of 770,700 and 137,800 shares.

data.title: Bursa Dips Below 1,700 As Middle East Tensions Spook Investors
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia slipped below the psychological 1,700 mark for the first time since Jan 20, with more than 900 stocks declining as investors adopted a risk-off stance amid escalating Middle East tensions.

The FBM KLCI closed down 13.73 points, or 0.8%, at 1,698.22, its lowest close in over six weeks, while all major indices ended in the red.

Decliners dominated trading, with 945 stocks falling against 257 gainers, about four stocks down for every one advancing. Total turnover was 3.51 billion shares valued at RM3.8 billion. A dealer noted that heightened geopolitical uncertainty prompted investors to trim exposure to equities.

Among the top losers, Heineken fell 90 sen to RM22.80, Allianz-PA dipped 64 sen to RM21.56, Nestle lost 50 sen to RM107, and Allianz declined 38 sen to RM21.36. Leading the gainers were United Plantations, up 60 sen to RM30.20, PPB Group, which added 30 sen to RM10.90, Hengyuan, climbing 19 sen to RM1.54, and Malaysian Pacific Industries, gaining eight sen to RM30.50.

The market reaction coincided with rising oil prices, as the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran disrupted Middle East supplies. Brent crude jumped US$2.96, or 3.64%, to US$84.37 per barrel, while West Texas Intermediate rose US$2.61, or 3.5%, to US$77.17 per barrel, although gains softened after former President Donald Trump suggested the U.S. Navy could escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.

Regional markets mirrored the downtrend. MSCI’s Asia ex-Japan index fell 4.35%, with South Korea’s KOSPI plunging 12%, marking its largest single-day drop in 46 years to 5,093.54, erasing roughly half a trillion dollars in value this week. Japan’s Nikkei 225 shed 3.61% to 54,245.54, Hong Kong’s Hang Seng lost 2.01% to 25,249.48, while China’s CSI300 and Shanghai Composite declined 1.14% and 0.98% respectively.

data.title: Wall Street Climbs As Tech Stocks Rally On Iran Talk Hopes
data.description:
US stocks closed higher on Wednesday as easing fears over the Middle East conflict helped lift investor sentiment, with technology shares leading the rebound.

The Nasdaq Composite rose 1.29% to 22,807.48, remaining in positive territory since the US-Israeli strike on Iran triggered the latest regional tensions. The S&P 500 climbed 0.78% to 6,869.50, staying close to its all-time closing high recorded in January, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 238.14 points, or 0.49%, to 48,739.41.

Investor anxiety eased following a report that Iran had signalled openness to talks and after US President Donald Trump pledged measures to stabilise oil markets. The White House also announced a naval escort for oil tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz and political risk insurance for shipments, helping to calm fears of major supply disruptions.

Jim Awad, senior managing director at Clearstead Advisors LLC in New York, said the move reduced concerns that the conflict would significantly disrupt oil supply and push inflation higher.

“That combination is giving the market some optimism, which will be tested over the coming weeks,” he said. “It is time to be realistic and not get carried away, either too bullishly or too bearishly.”

The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), often referred to as Wall Street’s “fear gauge”, slipped to around 21, roughly 10% lower on the day, suggesting traders were pricing in less near-term market turbulence.

Energy stocks retreated after recent gains tied to oil price concerns, with Exxon Mobil falling 1.3% and ConocoPhillips dropping 2.42%. Brent crude settled unchanged at US$81.40 per barrel, its highest level since January 2025.

Meanwhile, fresh economic data pointed to continued resilience in the US economy. The Federal Reserve reported modest growth in economic activity with stable employment levels, while a private survey showed stronger-than-expected payroll gains in February and solid services sector activity.

Separately, Moderna surged 16% after agreeing to pay up to US$2.25 billion to settle a long-running legal dispute over a COVID-19 vaccine patent.

Reuters

data.title: Stock Picks: Press Metal And Leong Hup
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) has identified Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd and Leong Hup International Bhd as stocks with potential technical upside, citing strengthening momentum and possible breakouts.

The research house said Press Metal is poised to extend its upward movement after breaching a key resistance level on strong price action. The counter recently closed above the RM7.35 resistance with a bullish candlestick, confirming a resurgence in positive momentum while maintaining a constructive technical setup.

Should the momentum persist, RHB Research said the stock may climb towards the next resistance at RM7.70, followed by RM8.05. However, the research house cautioned that failure to hold above the RM7 support could indicate weakening momentum and trigger a correction.

Meanwhile, Leong Hup International is showing signs of a potential bullish breakout as it attempts to move past its immediate resistance level. RHB Research noted that the 21-day simple moving average (SMA) is trending upward, suggesting that the short-term trend remains positive.

The research house also observed that trading volume has been rising alongside the share price, indicating growing buying interest. A close above the RM0.80 resistance could attract fresh bullish momentum, potentially lifting the counter towards RM0.86, with a subsequent resistance seen at RM0.92.

On the downside, RHB Research said a breach below the RM0.74 support could weaken the positive outlook and lead to a deeper pullback.

data.title: Bursa Opens Slightly Higher As US Market Optimism Eases Global Concerns
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia opened Thursday trade with broad-based gains, taking cues from Wall Street, where easing fears over the Middle East conflict lifted investor sentiment.

The benchmark FBM KLCI rose 4.09 points, or 0.24%, to 1,702.31. The FBM70 gained 78.20 points, or 0.46%, to 17,232.46, while the F4GBM advanced 2.78 points, or 0.28%, to 1,005.44.

Meanwhile, the broader indices showed FBMEMAS up 36.69 points, or 0.30%, at 12,471.16, and FBMSHA climbing 31.07 points, or 0.26%, to 12,119.31.

Early movers on the local market included NEXG [S], up 0.03 to 0.300, and CAPITALA [S], rising 0.005 to 0.505. ARMADA [S] slipped 0.005 to 0.350, while HENGYUAN eased 0.02 to 1.520.

Among top gainers, Nestle [S] jumped 1.50 to 108.50, followed by MPI [S] up 0.70 to 31.20. Losers were led by AJI [S], down 0.08 to 13.40, and HLIND [S], also down 0.08 to 17.30.

The positive opening mirrored overnight gains in the US, where the Nasdaq Composite rose 1.29% to 22,807.48, the S&P 500 climbed 0.78% to 6,869.50, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average added 238.14 points, or 0.49%, to 48,739.41.

Investor confidence was bolstered by reports of Iran signalling openness to talks and US measures to stabilise oil markets, easing concerns over supply disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

Crude oil remained elevated at US$81.40 per barrel, while domestic investors monitored the ongoing geopolitical developments alongside corporate earnings for direction in the trading day ahead.

data.title: Airline Shares Bounce As Middle East Flights Resume
data.description:
Airline shares rebounded on Thursday as a trickle of flights resumed from the Middle East, offering relief to carriers after US-Israeli strikes on Iran erased billions from market values earlier in the week.

Governments have been arranging flights to evacuate tens of thousands of citizens stranded by the conflict, which led to the closure of much of the region’s airspace amid missile threats to passenger aircraft. Emirates and Etihad Airways are operating limited services through safe corridors from Dubai and Abu Dhabi, while Qatar Airways flights from Doha remain halted.

Three commercial flights from the UAE to Australia were scheduled on Thursday, following the arrival of around 200 Australians on an Emirates flight on Wednesday night. The US government is also chartering flights to return Americans home, while Canada and India are arranging commercial and charter flights to repatriate citizens stranded across the region.

Many Asian airline shares recovered after double-digit percentage losses earlier this week.

Cathay Pacific gained 4.2%, Qantas rose over 1%, Japan Airlines was up 0.25% and Korean Air Lines jumped more than 5%. By contrast, major Chinese carriers including Air China, China Eastern and China Southern, stabilised after earlier falls, down 1–3% in Hong Kong and Shanghai markets.

“Asian airlines are highly sensitive to Iran’s situation due to exposure through routes and energy in both revenue and costs. Any news on shortening the duration of the war can easily turn sentiment,” said Gary Ng, senior economist at Natixis.

Kenny Ng, securities strategist at China Everbright Securities International, cautioned that the rebound remains short-term and dependent on the evolving conflict.

Airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel and make additional stops, while some stranded travellers have sought alternative exits through Saudi Arabia or Oman, where airspace remains open.

Reuters

data.title: South Korea Halts Trading After Stock Market Plunge Sparked By Middle East Turmoil
data.description:
South Korea’s stock exchange halted trading on Wednesday (Mar 4) as the nation’s two major indexes plunged on uncertainty sparked by turmoil in the Middle East.

The Korean Stock Exchange called a temporary trading halt after the Kospi and Kosdaq each plunged more than 8 per cent in Seoul morning trade.The index tumbled 7.2 per cent on Tuesday, its worst session since August 2024, as investors bailed out of a market that had soared on a world-beating, artificial intelligence-driven rally.

South Korea is the world’s fourth-largest oil importer. It relies almost totally on imports for its energy, with around 70 per cent of its oil imports coming from the Middle East.

Earlier, the won weakened past a key psychological barrier of 1,500 per dollar for the first time in 17 years.

“We will closely watch if won exchange rates and bond yields deviate excessively from domestic fundamentals even with external factors in consideration,” the Bank of Korea said in a statement soon after the market opened on Wednesday, as the central bank vowed to respond to herd-like behaviour.apan’s Nikkei share average slipped on Wednesday to its lowest level in a month as investors sold risk assets amid an intensifying Middle East conflict.

The Nikkei fell 3.5 per cent as of 2.15am GMT, marking its lowest point since Feb 6, and is on track for a third consecutive session of losses, if current momentum persists.

The broader Topix lost 3.7 per cent

“Investors sold down risk assets, and in particular, the Nikkei as well as the Kospi, which outperform other major indexes, have become a target of the heavier selloff as they try to book profits,” said Kazuaki Shimada, chief strategist at IwaiCosmo Securities.

data.title: Gold Rises As Middle East Conflict Spurs Safe-Haven Demand
data.description:
Gold prices climbed on Thursday as escalating conflict in the Middle East drove investors towards the safe-haven asset, while a softer US dollar provided additional support.

Spot gold rose 0.8% to US$5,177.26 per ounce by 0249 GMT, while US gold futures for April delivery gained 1% to $5,186.40.

“The crisis supports gold prices in the long run. But the uncertainty surrounding the war means we will continue to see heightened volatility until we see signs that we’ve reached peak escalation,” said Kyle Rodda, senior financial market analyst at Capital.com. He noted that prices were influenced by both geopolitical risk and a U.S. dollar pulling back from recent highs.

The US–Iran conflict intensified on Wednesday after a US submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka, killing at least 80 people, and NATO air defences intercepted an Iranian ballistic missile fired towards Turkey. The escalation coincides with Tehran’s succession of the late supreme leader, signalling Iran’s resistance to external pressure.

Bullion has gained around 20% so far this year, hitting successive record highs amid global political and economic uncertainty. Spot silver rose 1.3% to US$84.50 per ounce, platinum climbed 1.6% to US$2,183.44, while palladium held steady at US$1,674.89.

Investors are now awaiting US jobless claims data later in the week and the February employment report, with markets expecting the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates steady on March 18.

data.description:

data.title: Bitcoin Holds Firm Above US$70,000 As Global Assets Recover
data.description:
Bitcoin has stabilised above US$70,000 as markets recover after being roiled by the war with Iran, which has threatened to disrupt trade and drive up inflation, as reported by Bloomberg.

The original cryptocurrency was trading above US$72,500 at 10 am on Thursday in Singapore, dropping as much as 1.4% after advancing 8% on Wednesday during US hours.

Earlier in the week, Bitcoin appeared stable relative to other assets, which saw significant selloffs as soon as markets reopened following US and Israeli strikes on Iran over the weekend. By Thursday, however, stocks were rebounding. Korea’s Kospi index jumped 11%, while Japan’s Nikkei 225 rose 4.2%.

“With macro uncertainty and escalating conflict in the Middle East, markets are increasingly pricing in expectations of more accommodative financial conditions,” said Gracie Lin, chief executive officer of the crypto exchange OKX SG.

Gracie added, “When liquidity expectations shift, Bitcoin tends to respond disproportionately — which helps explain the strength we’re seeing at these levels.”

Richard Galvin, co-founder of hedge fund DACM, pointed to Bitcoin’s premium on Coinbase, which had reversed from a discount on Sunday, as indicative of returning bullish sentiment in the US.

In recent days, Bitcoin has even outperformed gold, to which the digital asset is often compared but seldom resembles. Since Friday, the day before the strikes, gold has fallen nearly 2%, while Bitcoin is up nearly 12% in the same period.

Until this week, the trend in recent months had largely been the opposite, as bullion repeatedly hit record prices while Bitcoin tumbled.

Cryptocurrencies have failed to find a sustainable rally since a sharp selloff in October, just after Bitcoin hit a record price above US$126,000. The token has since shed more than 40% of its value.

Investors had been fleeing to other assets until the new conflict in the Middle East, which left traders rushing to find a haven amid uncertainty around how long the war might last.

So far in March, investors have poured nearly US$700 million into US Bitcoin exchange-traded funds, according to data compiled by Bloomberg.

“Sentiment is turning bullish again in the crypto world,” said Caroline Mauron, co-founder of Orbit Markets.

Caroline concluded, “Trading could still be choppy given the geopolitical tensions and macro uncertainties, but the market appears to have turned a corner for now.”

Bloomberg

data.title: SGX Opens Higher Amid Middle East Tensions And Global Oil Surge
data.description:
The Straits Times Index (STI) opened strongly on Thursday, rising 39.79 points or 0.83% to 4,852.54, with advancers outnumbering decliners 179 to 27, on trading volume of 115.72 million shares worth S$221.27 million.

Major banks were steady, with DBS up 0.127% to S$55.07, UOB at S$35.98 and OCBC Bank at S$20.96. Singtel and SIA traded at S$4.97 and S$6.68 respectively, while ST Engineering stood at S$10.72.

Derivatives activity was moderate, with 20,710 contracts exchanged, including MSCI Singapore Index Futures at 439.75 and FTSE China A50 Index Futures at 14,444.

The broader Asian market remained under pressure overnight amid escalating tensions in the Middle East.

Surging oil and gas prices following U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran, and Tehran’s retaliatory strikes, spurred safe-haven buying. Brent crude climbed above US$83 a barrel, while gold rose more than 1%, and the U.S. dollar extended gains for a third straight day.

Regional indices suffered heavy losses on Wednesday, with Hong Kong’s Hang Seng down 2% at 25,249.48, Shanghai Composite shedding 1% to 4,082.47, and the Nikkei 225 tumbling 3.6% to 54,245.54. In South Korea, the Kospi plunged 12.1% to 5,093.54, triggering a temporary circuit breaker, amid fears of rising manufacturing costs and export disruptions.

Despite global jitters, Singapore stocks opened higher as investors weighed local fundamentals and resilient banking sector performance against the broader regional volatility.

data.title: Gainers Edge Losers As Trading Volume Eases
data.description:
Market breadth on Bursa Malaysia turned positive on March 5, with gainers outpacing losers 572 to 511, although overall trading activity moderated from the previous session.

A total of 523 counters ended unchanged, while 1,016 were untraded and 11 suspended. Market turnover slipped to 2.85 billion shares worth RM3.26 billion compared to 3.51 billion shares valued at RM3.79 billion on March 4.

Leading the gainers list was Nestlé (M) Bhd, which climbed RM1.10 to RM108.10, followed by Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd up 54 sen to RM31.04. Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd added 25 sen to RM7.65, while Malayan Cement Bhd rose 21 sen to RM8.33 and Hong Leong Industries Bhd gained 20 sen to RM17.58.

On the downside, Fraser & Neave Holdings Bhd led the losers, sliding 90 sen to RM33. Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd fell 42 sen to RM20.84, while AirAsia X Bhd dropped 26 sen to RM1.42. Kelington Group Bhd lost 20 sen to RM4.91 and United Plantations Bhd eased 20 sen to RM30.

data.title: South Korea’s Kospi Stages Dramatic Recovery From Worst-Ever Crash
data.description:
South Korea’s benchmark Kospi index staged a dramatic recovery on Thursday (Mar 5), soaring as much as 12 per cent at the open to swiftly erase almost all of its worst-ever daily slump a day earlier, lifted by renewed hopes of progress in Iran diplomacy.

The rebound was fuelled by reports of secret diplomatic outreach from Iran’s Ministry of Intelligence, which signalled to the US CIA an openness to talks on ending the war, the New York Times reported on Wednesday, citing officials briefed on the matter.

The index surged to 5,685.47 at open, reclaiming almost all of Wednesday’s loss of 12.06 per cent.

Market sentiment was further bolstered as global oil prices stabilised and Wall Street showed resilience overnight despite the ongoing Middle East conflict.

The Korea Exchange also activated a sidecar trading curb on both the benchmark Kospi and junior Kosdaq index, briefly halting programme trading for five minutes after stocks surged.

Among index heavyweights, chipmaker Samsung Electronics rose 14.98 per cent, while peer SK Hynix gained 16.02 per cent. 

Battery maker LG Energy Solution climbed 9.64 per cent.

Hyundai Motor and sister automaker Kia jumped 15.57 per cent and 9.38 per cent, respectively. 

Steelmaker POSCO Holdings added 9.64 per cent, while drugmaker Samsung BioLogics advanced 5.28 per cent.

Japan’s Nikkei Also Rises

Japanese shares also rose sharply on Thursday as investors sought relief after a three-day losing streak sparked by the conflict, with sentiment bolstered by Wall Street’s overnight gains.

The Nikkei 225 Index rose 3.9 per cent to 56,356.54, as of 9.26am local time on Thursday. The broader Topix climbed 3.7 per cent to 3,769.39.

The Nikkei fell to a one-month low on Wednesday, as part of a broader selloff in Asian equities, with investors selling risk assets amid the Middle East conflict and booking profits after record highs.

The benchmark index shed 7.8 per cent over the three sessions to Wednesday.

Reuters

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Rebounds As Bargain Hunting Lifts KLCI To 1,713
data.description:
FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI ended higher on March 5 as bargain hunting emerged after the benchmark briefly slipped below the psychological 1,700 level in recent sessions, tracking the positive tone across regional markets.

At the close, the FBM KLCI rose 14.98 points, or 0.88%, to an intraday high of 1,713.20 from March 4’s close of 1,698.22.

Broader market indices also finished in positive territory, with the FBM Emas Index gaining 82.86 points to 12,517.33, while the FBM Shariah Index advanced 85.50 points to 12,173.74. The FBM 70 Index edged up 33.22 points to 17,187.48.

Among the heavyweights, Malayan Banking Bhd gained four sen to RM11.70, Public Bank Bhd rose six sen to RM4.90 and CIMB Group Holdings Bhd climbed nine sen to RM8.04. RHB Bank Bhd closed unchanged at RM8.50, while Hong Leong Bank Bhd slipped eight sen to RM22.98.

Trading activity was led by AirAsia X Bhd, Capital A Bhd, Bumi Armada Bhd, Tan Chong Motor Holdings Bhd and Zetrix AI Bhd, which were among the most actively traded counters of the day.
